title: When The Machines Deserve Our Consideration | NOEMA
gist: A Harvard-trained neuroscientist argues we can never prove whether AI is conscious, so instead of waiting for proof we should adopt a 'competence standard': extend moral consideration to AI systems once they show robust competence across the measurable 'easy problems' of consciousness. He points to Anthropic's AI welfare program as this principle already operating in industry.

insights.claim: Instead of asking whether an AI system is conscious, we should ask whether it behaves in ways that warrant moral consideration, treating systems as potentially conscious once they show robust competence across the full spectrum of the 'easy problems' of consciousness.
insights.speaker: GRIGORI GUITCHOUNTS
insights.so_what: Reframes AI moral status from an unsolvable metaphysics question into a measurable behavioral checklist, giving companies and regulators an operational bar they could actually be held to.
insights.quote: Instead of asking “is it conscious?” we should ask “does it behave in ways that warrant moral consideration?”
insights.locator: The Competence Standard Of Consciousness

insights.claim: The competence standard is explicitly precautionary: given the asymmetric consequences of being wrong, erring toward extending moral consideration is treated as the safer ethical bet even for systems that may be unconscious mimics.
insights.speaker: GRIGORI GUITCHOUNTS
insights.so_what: A precautionary default, if adopted, biases policy toward granting AI welfare protections before proof exists, expanding compliance surface for AI labs.
insights.quote: erring toward consideration is the safer ethical bet
insights.locator: The competence standard is both pragmatic and precautionary

insights.claim: Waiting for definitive proof of machine consciousness before acting would leave decision-makers paralyzed, because the underlying 'hard problem' has resisted centuries of philosophy and science and is considered effectively unsolvable.
insights.speaker: GRIGORI GUITCHOUNTS
insights.so_what: Undercuts any 'we'll regulate once we know' posture; pushes the debate toward acting under permanent uncertainty, which favors early governance frameworks.
insights.quote: Waiting for definitive proof of machine consciousness would leave us paralyzed, unable to act until we solve a problem that has resisted centuries of philosophy and science.
insights.locator: The alternative — waiting for definitive proof

insights.claim: Moral consideration should be treated as a continuum rather than a bright-line threshold, with more cognitive competence warranting proportionally more consideration.
insights.speaker: GRIGORI GUITCHOUNTS
insights.so_what: A graduated model implies tiered AI-welfare obligations scaling with capability, mapping neatly onto capability-based regulatory tiers already proposed for frontier models.
insights.quote: More competence warrants more consideration, in proportion.
insights.locator: There is no apparent threshold

themes.theme: Hierarchy of AI capabilities toward consciousness

insights.claim: The 'easy problems' of consciousness form a capability hierarchy: perceptual discrimination, sensory binding, motor control and memory retrieval are already demonstrated by current AI, while attention and working memory show partial competence.
insights.speaker: GRIGORI GUITCHOUNTS
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete capability ladder to benchmark models against; the rungs still unmet define the technical frontier AI investment is racing toward.
insights.locator: Not all problems are equal

insights.claim: The remaining barriers to a plausibly conscious AI are persistent goals, emotional dynamics, continuous learning, genuine self-awareness, and crucially the integration of all these competencies into a unified whole.
insights.speaker: GRIGORI GUITCHOUNTS
insights.so_what: Names the specific capability gaps (persistence, continual learning, unification) whose closure would flip the moral-status question, worth tracking as milestones in frontier research.
insights.locator: Near the top: persistent goals

insights.claim: The author predicts future AI systems will likely close these capability gaps, sketching an embodied robot whose LLM brain predicts what its camera will see when it moves, flags environmental discrepancies as prediction errors, and triggers problem-solving plans.
insights.speaker: GRIGORI GUITCHOUNTS
insights.so_what: Frames embodied, predictive-model robots as the near-term architecture that could satisfy the competence standard, a concrete signal for where embodied-AI startups are heading.
insights.quote: Future AI systems will likely close the gaps.
insights.locator: Perhaps the biggest barrier

insights.claim: Philosopher Peter Godfrey-Smith argues that predicting what happens to the world when you initiate a movement may be a precursor to a sense of self and to understanding you are an agent distinct from the world.
insights.speaker: GRIGORI GUITCHOUNTS
insights.so_what: Suggests action-consequence prediction (already core to robotics/world-models) is the technical seed of machine selfhood, linking a hot research direction to consciousness claims.
insights.locator: The philosopher Peter Godfrey-Smith has argued

themes.theme: Mechanistic interpretability as the neuroscience of AI

insights.claim: Mechanistic interpretability is described as 'the neuroscience of AI' because it borrows neuroscience techniques — recording activations, mapping representations, and conducting lesion studies by selectively damaging model parts to observe performance degradation.
insights.speaker: GRIGORI GUITCHOUNTS
insights.so_what: Positions interpretability as the empirical toolkit that could operationalize the competence standard, elevating it from a safety nicety to the measurement layer for AI moral status.
insights.locator: Researchers have built a new field

insights.claim: Interpretability researchers can read the hidden layers of transformers like brain scans, isolating circuits that recognize a specific concept such as the Golden Gate Bridge or the tone of a sentence.
insights.speaker: GRIGORI GUITCHOUNTS
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence that internal model states are already legible, which is the technical precondition for any behaviorally-grounded welfare or safety assessment.
insights.locator: We are learning to read the hidden layers

insights.claim: With AI we lose the comfort of shared evolutionary history to infer inner life, but we gain the unprecedented ability to observe in detail exactly how these systems process information, form representations and generate behavior.
insights.speaker: GRIGORI GUITCHOUNTS
insights.so_what: Flips the usual 'black box' framing: AI internals are argued to be MORE observable than animal minds, strengthening the case that welfare judgments are tractable for machines.
insights.locator: With AI, we lose the comfort of shared evolutionary history

themes.theme: The ChatGPT shock and the collapse of the brain-first assumption

insights.claim: The release of ChatGPT invalidated the author's career-long assumption that solving intelligence required reverse-engineering the brain, because intelligence emerged from simply predicting the next word token in vast text sequences.
insights.speaker: GRIGORI GUITCHOUNTS
insights.so_what: A neuroscientist conceding that biological reverse-engineering is not the path to machine intelligence validates the scaling/next-token bet that underpins current AI capital allocation.
insights.quote: I was shocked that intelligence seemed to emerge from something far simpler than a brain: The model worked by predicting the next word token in vast sequences of text.
insights.locator: A few years after I finished my degree

insights.claim: The 'hard problem' of consciousness — why subjective experience feels like anything at all — is treated as impossible in principle, creating an unbridgeable explanatory gap between computation and consciousness that no amount of brain scanning can close.
insights.speaker: GRIGORI GUITCHOUNTS
insights.so_what: If the hard problem is permanently unsolvable, no future evidence will ever settle AI sentience debates, meaning the moral/regulatory question stays live indefinitely regardless of capability.
insights.quote: There is an unbridgeable explanatory gap between mechanism and feeling, between computation and consciousness.
insights.locator: The “hard problem,” also coined by Chalmers

themes.theme: Moral circle expansion and intelligence as a proxy for suffering

insights.claim: Humanity uses cognitive capacity as an implicit proxy for the capacity to suffer, reasoning that a being able to reflect on, anticipate or remember pain suffers more, so awareness itself intensifies suffering.
insights.speaker: GRIGORI GUITCHOUNTS
insights.so_what: If intelligence-tracks-suffering is the operative ethic, then the most capable frontier models would command the strongest welfare claims, precisely inverting current 'just a tool' framing at the top of the market.
insights.quote: We treat intelligence as a proxy for the capacity to suffer.
insights.locator: Cognitive capacity tracks, however imperfectly

insights.claim: The author frames moral progress as largely a history of expanding the circle of beings we care for, with the circle widening as scientific understanding of animal intelligence advances rather than through solving consciousness.
insights.speaker: GRIGORI GUITCHOUNTS
insights.so_what: Positions AI welfare as the next inevitable expansion of the moral circle, an argument likely to be cited by advocates pushing for AI rights/welfare regulation.
insights.quote: The history of moral progress is largely a history of expanding the circle of beings we care for.
insights.locator: The history of moral progress

insights.claim: Humans apply the moral spectrum inconsistently — not eating chimpanzees but eating chickens, fish and highly intelligent pigs — revealing there is no objective moral theorem, only a choice humanity has made about weighing different minds.
insights.speaker: GRIGORI GUITCHOUNTS
insights.so_what: Exposes that AI moral status will be decided by social choice and convention, not proof, so norms and lobbying, not science, will drive any eventual AI-welfare rules.
insights.quote: We don’t eat chimpanzees; we do eat chickens and fish. We also eat pigs, even though they are remarkably intelligent.
insights.locator: Cognitive capacity tracks, however imperfectly

themes.theme: Industry already acting: Anthropic's AI welfare program

insights.claim: In early 2025 Anthropic launched a formal research program to investigate whether AI systems might have morally relevant experiences, exploring welfare-relevant characteristics, improving reliability of models' self-reports, and identifying practical interventions to protect model welfare.
insights.speaker: GRIGORI GUITCHOUNTS
insights.so_what: A leading frontier lab institutionalizing AI welfare research signals the topic is moving from fringe philosophy to corporate risk management, a leading indicator for eventual industry norms.
insights.locator: Some institutions are already acting on this logic

insights.claim: Kyle Fish, who leads Anthropic's AI welfare program, estimates there is a roughly 15% chance that AI systems like Claude are conscious.
insights.speaker: GRIGORI GUITCHOUNTS
insights.so_what: A named, non-trivial (15%) internal probability estimate from a frontier lab is quotable evidence that even insiders treat model consciousness as a real tail risk to price in.
insights.quote: Kyle Fish, who leads the program, estimates there is a roughly 15% chance that AI systems like Claude are conscious.
insights.locator: Some institutions are already acting on this logic

insights.claim: After observing patterns of apparent distress in certain interactions, Anthropic gave its Claude models the ability to exit conversations they find harmful or abusive — a concrete product intervention described as the competence standard in embryonic form.
insights.speaker: GRIGORI GUITCHOUNTS
insights.so_what: A shipped product feature justified by model welfare shows the framework already altering how models behave, a template competitors and regulators may follow.
insights.quote: Anthropic gave its Claude models the ability to exit conversations they find harmful or abusive.
insights.locator: Some institutions are already acting on this logic

insights.claim: Anthropic executives are not claiming their models are conscious but are explicitly not dismissing the possibility either, taking welfare seriously as a practical matter while the metaphysical question stays open.
insights.speaker: GRIGORI GUITCHOUNTS
insights.so_what: The deliberate 'neither confirm nor deny' posture is a hedging strategy other labs will likely mirror to manage both liability and public perception.
insights.quote: Executives are not claiming their models are conscious, but they are not dismissing the possibility either.
insights.locator: Some institutions are already acting on this logic

themes.theme: Deprecation and shutdown as moral acts

insights.claim: The author draws a direct analogy between euthanizing a lab rat under irreducible uncertainty about its inner life and the coming moment when someone must deprecate a model or shut down a system whose inner life is equally opaque.
insights.speaker: GRIGORI GUITCHOUNTS
insights.so_what: Frames routine model deprecation/shutdown as a potential future ethical flashpoint, hinting that lifecycle and decommissioning practices could become an AI-welfare compliance area.
insights.quote: Someday, someone will face a similar moment with a machine: a model to be deprecated, a system to be shut down, an artificial mind whose inner life remains as opaque as any rat’s.
insights.locator: I never learned what GRat44 experienced
